name: Valverde del Camino and La Palma del Condado Loop
name_original: Bonares, Niebla, Valverde del Camino, La Palma del Condado, Rociana del Condado – ruta circular
description: This 64.3-mile (103.5 km) moderate road cycling route connects Bonares, Niebla, and other towns in Huelva province.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Bonares, located in the province of Huelva, Spain, traverse diverse landscapes. The region features varied terrain, including dedicated cycling infrastructure like the "Green Way," which offers both paved and dirt sections. Cyclists can experience shady forests in mountainous areas, expansive holm oak pastures, and routes that pass by ancient mines. This varied topography provides a dynamic backdrop for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Bonares The most popular road cycling route is Niebla, Trigueros, and San Juan Loop, a 26.9 miles (43.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 57 minutes to complete. This easy route offers a circuit through local towns. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Valverde del Camino and La Palma del Condado Loop, a moderate 64.3 miles (103.5 km) path. This longer route covers varied scenery, including agricultural areas and open countryside. Local road cyclists also love the El Berrocal Viewpoint – Villarrasa loop from Villarrasa, a 57.9 miles (93.2 km) trail leading through areas with notable viewpoints, often completed in about 4 hours 31 minutes. Road cycling around Bonares is defined by diverse scenery, including shady forests, holm oak pastures, and varied natural features. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging routes with significant elevation gain. The routes in Bonares are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 1 review. More than 150 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Bonares's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Bonares?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes around Bonares, offering a variety of distances and difficulty levels to explore the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Bonares?
A: Road cycling in Bonares features diverse terrain, from dedicated cycling infrastructure like the "Green Way" which includes both paved and dirt sections, to routes passing through shady forests in mountainous areas, expansive holm oak pastures, and even ancient mines. You'll find a dynamic backdrop for your rides.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy ride?
A: Yes, Bonares offers several easy road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991776253/niebla-trigueros-and-san-juan-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Niebla, Trigueros, and San Juan Loop&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 26.9-mile (43.2 km) trail that provides a pleasant circuit through local towns.

Q: What about more challenging road cycling routes in Bonares?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, Bonares and the surrounding Huelva province offer routes with significant elevation gain. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122549" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Berrocal Viewpoint – Villarrasa loop from Villarrasa&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 57.9-mile (93.2 km) trail with notable viewpoints and climbs.

Q: What historical landmarks or points of interest can I see while road cycling near Bonares?
A: The region around Bonares is rich in history. You can encounter landmarks such as the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4525837" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Niebla Castle&lt;/a&gt;, the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/389426" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcázar or Castle of the Guzmáns&lt;/a&gt;, and the well-preserved &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/386978" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Bridge 🌉 Niebla&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes pass close to these historical sites, allowing for cultural exploration.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints or natural features to look out for?
A: Absolutely. Road cycling routes in Bonares often lead through areas with diverse natural beauty, including shady forests, expansive holm oak pastures, and routes that pass by ancient mines. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122549" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Berrocal Viewpoint – Villarrasa loop from Villarrasa&lt;/a&gt;, for example, is known for its notable viewpoints.

Q: Is there dedicated cycling infrastructure in Bonares?
A: Yes, Bonares features dedicated cycling infrastructure, notably the "Green Way." This track is designed for cyclists and walkers, offering both paved and dirt sections to minimize traffic encounters and provide a safer riding experience.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Bonares?
A: While specific family-friendly routes are not detailed, the presence of easy routes and dedicated infrastructure like the "Green Way" suggests that certain sections or shorter loops could be suitable for families, especially those with some cycling experience. Always check the route difficulty and terrain before heading out with children.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Many of the road cycling routes around Bonares are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991776253/niebla-trigueros-and-san-juan-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Niebla, Trigueros, and San Juan Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991324522/valverde-del-camino-and-la-palma-del-condado-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino and La Palma del Condado Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Bonares?
A: The road cycling routes in Bonares are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from reviewers. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain and the opportunity to explore the diverse scenery of the Huelva province.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Bonares?
A: Given its location in Huelva, Spain, Bonares generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn typically provide mild temperatures, making them ideal. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended, while winters are generally mild but can have occasional rain.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Bonares?
A: While specific parking locations for each trailhead are not detailed, Bonares and nearby towns typically offer public parking areas. It's advisable to check local maps or use navigation apps to locate convenient parking near your chosen starting point.

Q: Are there longer road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for those seeking longer distances, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991324522/valverde-del-camino-and-la-palma-del-condado-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino and La Palma del Condado Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 64.3-mile (103.5 km) path that covers varied scenery, including agricultural areas and open countryside, perfect for a longer day out.
tips: The historic almost 2000 years old Puente Romano crosses the Tinto river into Niebla
Alcázar o Castillo de los Guzmánes has some remnants of Roman construction, its 2000 years-old concrete mix survives better than contemporary concrete
Historic town with impressive ruins from Roman times, architecture from Arab occupations, and later.
Medieval southeastern city gate with mudejar elements

One of the most beautiful in the province of Huelva, the Plaza de España in La Palma del Condado is the social center of this picturesque town. Surrounded by unique buildings such as the Casa de los Arcos, the Town Hall and the Church of San Juan Bautista, this beautiful place is chosen to have a good time at sunset on summer nights.

Its full name is the Church of Nuestra Señora de las Angustias, and it is located in the town of Villarrasa, a small town in Huelva with barely 2,300 inhabitants, on the street to which it has given the same name, Calle Angustias. This architectural work has participated in the filming of Western movies in which actors such as Mario Girotti, better known as Terence Hill, have acted.
It is an architectural construction from the 16th century that takes advantage of the foundations of previous buildings, the oldest part being the central nave. In later centuries the rest of the work was completed, incorporating Renaissance and Baroque elements, mainly on the façade, cornices, dome and dressing room.
Inside, it preserves a carving of Ntra. Sra. De las Angustias, a piety sculpted by Castillo Lastrucci in 1948, which replaces the one lost in 1936. Currently the church is also the headquarters of the Brotherhood of the Holy Christ of Goodness, which It constitutes one of the most emblematic images of the town of Villarrasa along with La Borriquita.
This is one of the few buildings in the town of Villarrasa that remained standing after the famous earthquake in Lisbon that hit practically the entire province of Huelva, leaving everything demolished in its path. In 2009 it underwent restoration work due to its poor condition, and that is why its external appearance has changed due to the color of the paint used and the brick reinforcements.

faqs.question: Where does this racebike route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: As a circular route connecting several towns, you can begin your ride in any of the mentioned locations: Bonares, Niebla, Valverde del Camino, La Palma del Condado, or Rociana del Condado. For parking, look for public parking areas within these towns. Specific trailhead parking isn't designated as it's a road cycling route connecting settlements.

faqs.question: Is this a loop route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is designed as a circular loop, allowing you to start and finish in the same location after completing the full circuit through Bonares, Niebla, Valverde del Camino, La Palma del Condado, and Rociana del Condado.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. While the region includes some Vías Verdes with gentler inclines, this specific racebike route covers a distance of approximately 103.5 km with an elevation gain of about 815 meters, suggesting a mix of rolling hills and some more challenging ascents on local roads. It requires a good level of fitness for racebike cycling.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: You'll primarily be cycling on paved roads suitable for a racebike. The terrain in this part of Huelva province can vary, featuring rolling hills and some more challenging ascents, especially when connecting the towns. While some Vías Verdes in the region are flatter, this route is likely to offer a diverse road cycling experience.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these periods, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter in Andalusia.

faqs.question: What historical and natural sights will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a rich blend of historical and natural sights. You'll pass by the impressive Niebla Castle and explore the historic town of Niebla itself, including the Puerta del Buey. Other highlights include the Roman bridge over the Tinto river, Plaza de España - La Palma del Condado, and the religious building Our Lady of Sorrows.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with any other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with several other paths. Notably, it briefly follows parts of the Camino de Santiago del Sur, Transandalus, and the Vía Verde de los Molinos del Agua. You may also encounter smaller segments of routes like the Ruta del Aceite de Beas.

faqs.question: Is this racebike route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, distance of over 100 km, and elevation gain, this route is generally not recommended for beginners or young families. It is better suited for experienced racebike cyclists with good fitness levels who are comfortable with longer distances and varied road terrain.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this route?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route connecting towns, there are no specific restrictions against dogs. However, dogs would need to be transported in a suitable bike trailer or carried, and local leash laws and regulations in the towns you pass through should always be observed.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or have to pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road cycling route. You can freely access and enjoy the ride through the towns and connecting roads.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for refreshments or accommodation along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns including Bonares, Niebla, Valverde del Camino, La Palma del Condado, and Rociana del Condado, you will find numerous opportunities for refreshments, meals, and accommodation. These towns offer cafes, restaurants, and various lodging options.
